---
title: "WHAT WAS LOST: tracing unmet demand in contraceptive supply chains"
description: "EURO 2025 Leeds, UK"
author: "Harsha Halgamuwe Hewage"
date: "2025-06-24"
image: "cover.png"
categories:
  - Forecasting
  - Inventory
  - Loss Sales
  - R
  - Tobit Kalman Filter
  - Conformal Prediction
link-external-newwindow: true
---

```{=html}
<!-- Add Font Awesome for icons -->
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/font-awesome/6.4.0/css/all.min.css">


<div class="hero-section">
  <h1 class="title">WHAT WAS LOST: tracing unmet demand in contraceptive supply chains</h1>
  <p class="description">EURO 2025 Leeds, UK</p>
  <p class="author-date">Harsha Halgamuwe Hewage | 2025-06-24</p>
  <div class="cta-buttons">
    <a href="slides/euro_leeds_25.html" target="_blank" class="btn-custom btn-primary">
      <i class="fas fa-bookmark"></i> View Slides
    </a>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="content-section">
  <h2>Context</h2>
  <p>At the <strong>EURO 2025 conference</strong> in Leeds, we present our work on reconstructing true demand in family planning supply chains—where stockouts routinely censor observed data and distort decision-making. Standard forecasting tools treat these absences as lack of demand, leading to understocking and reinforcing supply failure.</p>
  <p>We introduce a novel approach that integrates a Truncated Conformal Kalman Filter (TCKF) with simulation-based inventory evaluation. By correcting for both partial and full censorship and layering conformal prediction for distribution-free uncertainty, we recover latent demand more accurately and translate it into better ordering policies.</p>
  <p>Through synthetic experiments and real data application, we show how ignoring censored demand underestimates both need and risk. Our results point toward a scalable framework for inventory management in fragile public health systems—where demand isn’t lost, just buried under broken assumptions.</p>
  <p>Because “zero demand” doesn’t mean “zero need”—it often just means the shelves were empty.</p>
</div>
```
